extra.reasoning: rec_drug_a is set true only by r5, whose condition is exactly (seizure_history AND pregnancy AND polypharmacy); otherwise it defaults false. So rec_drug_a is true iff all three of seizure_history, pregnancy, polypharmacy hold. rec_drug_b is set true by r1 (priority 50, condition seizure_history) but can be set false by r3 (priority 90, condition seizure_history AND pregnancy AND polypharmacy). For the conflict to occur, rec_drug_a must be true, which requires (seizure_history AND pregnancy AND polypharmacy). But that is exactly r3's condition, and r3 (priority 90) outranks r1 (priority 50) for output rec_drug_b, forcing rec_drug_b := false. Thus in every input where rec_drug_a is true, rec_drug_b is false. Conversely, when rec_drug_b is true, seizure_history is true but at least one of pregnancy/polypharmacy is false, so r5's condition fails and rec_drug_a defaults false. The other inputs (hepatic_impairment, asthma, qt_prolongation) affect only rec_drug_c and are irrelevant. Enumerating: (1) seizure_history=F: a=F,b=F. (2) seizure_history=T but not all three: a=F,b=T. (3) all three true: a=T, b=F (r3 overrides r1). In no case are rec_drug_a and rec_drug_b jointly true, so the mutual-exclusion property holds across the entire input space.
